coredata.dc:title: Green Synthesis of carbon quantum dots for enhancing photocatalytic activity: Hydrogen/oxygen evolution and dye photodegradation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Backes, Claudio W.
Reis, Felipe B.
Strapasson, Guilherme B.
Assis, Marcelo
Longo, Elson
Weibel, Daniel E.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have recently attracted attention across various fields due to their small size, high conductivity, fluorescence emission, low toxicity, and other desirable characteristics. In this study, highly fluorescent CQDs with an average diameter of 3.7 nm were prepared via microwave irradiation using a standard commercial microwave oven and glycerol as solvent. Several cation promoters were examined for CQD synthesis, with copper ions ultimately chosen for comprehensive characterization and application. The CQDs were impregnated onto both commercially available and microwave-synthesized TiO2 nanoparticles. The photocatalytic activity was evaluated with respect to the hydrogen and oxygen generation. Under the employed conditions, the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) exhibited over 12 times higher efficiency than the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). The enhanced OER activity is attributed to the high electronic conductivity of the small Cu doped CQDs@TiO2 (Cu-CQDs@TiO2) facilitating an efficient electron transfer for the OER . Visible light activity (λ ≥ 400 nm) was demonstrated by photodegradation of the indigo carmine (IC) solution used as a model pollutant. Irradiation in the presence of the Cu-CQDs@TiO2 photocatalyst resulted in complete degradation of the dye in less than 3 hours. The results presented here provide a promising methodology for designing high-performance photocatalysts based on environmentally friendly CQD syntheses. Crucial applications, from renewable energy production to environmental remediation, will benefit from strategies using the carbon abundance on Earth.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Microwave
Photocatalysis
Hydrogen
Oxygen
Water splitting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Carbon quantum dots and TiO2 nanoparticles syntheses by microwave irradiation

Assis acknowledges the Ministerio de Ciencia e Innovaci\u00F3n (Spain) for the Juan de la Cierva fellowship with code JDC2022-049934-I.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Fluorescence emission spectra of the CQDs solutions prepared with several cation promoters. The counterion was SO4 2- in all the cases. (b) PL spectra of Cu-doped CQDs prepared with different concentrations of CuSO4. (c) Typical photographs of the solutions after syntheses via MW under natural light (top) and 365 nm irradiation (bottom) [CuSO4]: 2.5 mmol/L. MW irradiation time: 130 s. λexc: 390 nm.
Fluorescence emission spectra of the Cu-CQDs at varying excitation wavelength. [CuSO4]: 2.5 mmol/L.
Characterization of the prepared CQDs: (a) Typical TEM image of the as prepared CQDs, (b) EDS analysis of the regions in Fig. 3a. (c-d) HRTEM of a local lattice amplification of a single CQD.

XPS characterization of the prepared Cu-CQDs@P25TiO2: (a) Survey spectrum of the TiO2P25@CQDs, (b) C 1 s deconvolution signal. (c) O 1 s region, and (d) Ti 2p spectrum. TiO2 P25 was impregnated with Cu-CQDs.
Tauc Plots showing bandgap energies obtained from UV–visible DRS spectra of TiO2 NPs prepared via MW irradiation (Cu-CQDs@MWTiO2) at two different TiO2MW/CQDs ratios.

Hydrogen and Oxygen evolutions rates using Cu-CQDs@TiO2 nanocomposites system under UV-Vis irradiation. H2 evolution on: (a) commercial TiO2 P25 NPs impregnated with Cu-doped CQDs (Cu-CQDs@P25TiO2), (b) TiO2 NPs prepared by MW and impregnated with Cu-doped CQDs (Cu-CQDs@MWTiO2). (c) O2 evolution on (Cu-CQDs@P25TiO2).

Oxygen and Hydrogen (inset) photogeneration experiments under UV–vis and visible radiation using the prepared Cu-CQDs@P25TiO2 photocatalyst. The runs were carried out with only deionized water. After each cycle the reactor was deaerated with nitrogen for 30 minutes prior to a new irradiation.

Photodegradation of an indigo carmine solution vs. time using Cu-CQDs@P25TiO2 under irradiation with only visible light (λ ≥ 400 nm). IC concentration: 30 ppm. The IC solution was also irradiated with only visible light in the absence of the photocatalyst.
(a) Schematic representation for the syntheses of Carbon Quantum Dots by microwave irradiation and (b) their impregnation on TiO2 nanoparticles.
Hydrogen and Oxygen evolution rates using Cu-CQDs@P25TiO2 nanocomposites under UV-Vis irradiation. Commercial TiO2 P25 NPs were impregnated with Cu-doped CQDs. Data obtained from Fig. 6a and c.

head.ce:title: Green Synthesis of carbon quantum dots for enhancing photocatalytic activity: Hydrogen/oxygen evolution and dye photodegradation

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CDMF, Federal University of Sao Carlos - UFSCar, P.O. Box 676, 13565e905,São Carlos, São Paulo, Brazil

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have recently attracted attention across various fields due to their small size, high conductivity, fluorescence emission, low toxicity, and other desirable characteristics. In this study, highly fluorescent CQDs with an average diameter of 3.7 nm were prepared via microwave irradiation using a standard commercial microwave oven and glycerol as solvent. Several cation promoters were examined for CQD synthesis, with copper ions ultimately chosen for comprehensive characterization and application. The CQDs were impregnated onto both commercially available and microwave-synthesized TiO2 nanoparticles. The photocatalytic activity was evaluated with respect to the hydrogen and oxygen generation. Under the employed conditions, the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) exhibited over 12 times higher efficiency than the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). The enhanced OER activity is attributed to the high electronic conductivity of the small Cu doped CQDs@TiO2 (Cu-CQDs@TiO2) facilitating an efficient electron transfer for the OER . Visible light activity (λ ≥ 400 nm) was demonstrated by photodegradation of the indigo carmine (IC) solution used as a model pollutant. Irradiation in the presence of the Cu-CQDs@TiO2 photocatalyst resulted in complete degradation of the dye in less than 3 hours. The results presented here provide a promising methodology for designing high-performance photocatalysts based on environmentally friendly CQD syntheses. Crucial applications, from renewable energy production to environmental remediation, will benefit from strategies using the carbon abundance on Earth.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The seminal discovery of the water splitting (WS) reaction induced by light, yielding hydrogen and oxygen by Honda and Fujishima in 1972 marked an actual possibility to harvest solar light to produce green fuels [1]. Since then the development of thousands of titanium dioxide (TiO2) semiconductor materials has been studied owing to its chemical stability, straightforward synthesis, Earth abundance, and non-toxicity. However, it major drawback lies in the relatively low catalytic efficiency for H2 and O2 evolution.

TiO2 composite nanostructures have exhibited higher photocatalytic efficiency compared to bare TiO2 for hydrogen evolution [2–4]. In particular, the sensitization of TiO2 with carbon structures, such as carbon nanotubes, fullerenes, graphenes, and notably carbon quantum dots (CQDs), has garnered attention due to carbon abundance on Earth. CQDs have recently attracted increasing interest owing to their visible light absorption capacity, quantum confinement effects, multiple exciton generation, luminescent properties and efficient energy transfer [5,6]. Many CQDs syntheses exist, such as the top-down and bottom-up methodologies. The bottom-up approach tends to be simpler than the top-down one. It involves the preparation of CDQs from small organic precursors such as glucose/urea [7], citric acid/o-phenylenediamine [8], and other methods through a sequence of chemical reactions. The incorporation of ion metals in the CQDs preparation has also called the researcher attentions as a way of avoiding the use of any nonmetallic co-dopant. Heteroatom doped CQDs showed in general higher photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield in relation to un-doped ones. Probably, the first report on doping CQDs with a heavy metal without the presence of nonmetallic co-dopant was carried out using a hydrothermal method with sodium citrate and cuprous chloride as precursors [9]. That work showed the excellent fluorescent properties of Cu-CQDs and their selectivity for fast detection of Fe3+ (aq) for potential biomedical applications. Other metals have been used for the synthesis of CQDs, for example, iron and nitrogen co-doped CQDs were synthesized through a solvothermal method assisted by microwave irradiation [10]. In another report, Mg-N co-doped CQDs were prepared using an aqueous solution containing ethylenediamine and heated at 200 °C for 3 h by hydrothermal method [11].

Traditional preparation methods prevalent in many fields and particularly in the photocatalytic generation of hydrogen, often lack environmental friendliness [12]. In this sense, microwave (MW)-assisted chemistry is an efficient mode for rapid and homogeneous heating of the reaction mixtures by direct dipolar coupling of the MW energy with polar solvents and chemicals [12–14]. Several recent works have shown the benefits of the use of MW to produce specific nanomaterials with high crystalinity and small size. The preparation of FeNi Prussian blue analogue nanocrystals with about 10 nm size [15], cobalt oxide decorated carbon spheres for dye photocatalytic degradation and hydrogen photogeneration [16], CuS/ZnCdS heterojunction for efficient photocatalytic hydrogen evolution [17], CdS nanocrystals on a porous biomass carbon derived from the waste bagasse for hydrogen generation under solar light [18], are some of the examples. The applications of MW extend also to other fields, such as sensors for detection of hydrogen peroxide [19,20], melamine contamination [19] and photodegradation of dyes [21,22]. Our research group has also demonstrated the benefits of use MW for the preparation of small size photocatalyst nanostructures for hydrogen evolution and degradation of dyes [4,23,24].

Several techniques for the synthesis of CQDs using a variety of chemicals have been reported, such as arc-discharge, laser ablation, hydrothermal, plasma, ultrason, microwave, chemical exfoliation, pyrolysis, precipitation, and others [6]. Each method has its own advantages and limitations. The main advantages of MW compared to other techniques are a green chemistry process, use of low-toxic chemicals and solvents, such as water, simple chemicals and plant extracts and high product yields. In particular, the growth of inorganic nanostructures is highly sensitive to the reaction conditions and MW offers a benefit from the efficient and controlled heating by MW irradiation [25]. The absorption of MW energy depends on the polarity of the solvents that can couple with MW irradiation [26,27]. This coupling heats the reaction mixtures as a consequence of dipolar polarization and ionic conduction [25]. Glycerol and water have high dielectric constants of 46.5 and 80 at 20 0C respectively and glycerol is a by-product of the biodiesel. There is crude glycerol in surplus on the market which creates new challenges in terms of its uses [28]. It is estimated that during the production of 100 kg of biodiesel 10 kg of glycerol is generated [29]. In Brazil the agribusiness is the largest economic sector in the country representing about 25 % of the Brazilian gross domestic product including production, consumption, industry, and services [30].

In that context, here are presented green preparation methods of CQDs that were impregnated onto commercial and MW-prepared TiO2 nanoparticles (NPs). The use of MW irradiation coupled to glycerol/water solutions containing metal as promoters, leads in several cases to efficient CQDs synthesis. In the present work, copper ions were finally chosen for comprehensive characterization and application. Analysis of High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) and Energy-Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDX) data suggests the formation of Cu-doped CQDs. The Cu doped CQDs@TiO2 (Cu-CQDs@TiO2) photocatalysts were used for hydrogen and oxygen evolution under UV–vis irradiation, and degradation of the indigo carmine (IC) with only visible light illumination (λ ≥ 400 nm). The results presented here provide a promising methodology for designing high-performance photocatalysts for the generation of clean oxygen and hydrogen using environmentally friendly CQD and TiO2 nanoparticle syntheses.

Titanium (IV) bis (ammonium lactate) dihydroxide solution (TALH), 50 wt% in H2O, ammonium hydroxide, was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Bi-distilled glycerol and methanol were purchased from Casa da Química. Brazil. Titanium dioxide NPs powder, AEROXIDE® TiO2 P25 from EVONIK (a mixture of about 75 % anatase and 25 % rutile) was received from Degussa Corporation. Etanol (95 %), Glycerol (99 %), CuSO4.5 H2O (97 %), FeSO4 (99 %), Na2SO4 (99 %) and ZnSO4 (99 %) were obtained from Dinâmica, Brazil. Fe2(SO4)3 (95 %) and MgSO4 (98 %) were acquired from Synth and Êxodo, Brazil, respectively. Indigo Carmine (IC) (100 %) was obtained from Merck, Brazil.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon quantum dots and TiO2 nanoparticles syntheses by microwave irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs and TiO2 nanoparticles were synthesized by microwave irradiation. CDQs were prepared using pure glycerol as a solvent and reducing agent. A typical synthesis consisted of the addition of 0.1 mL of the metal solution (0.755 mol/L) to 15 mL of Glycerol. The solution was magnetic stirring for homogenization and then introduced into a homemade Teflon reactor for MW irradiation. The MW was carried out using a commercial Panasonic Microwave oven (1600 W) at high power for 130 s. After the synthesis, 1 g of the final solution was diluted with 50 mL of deionized water for fluorescence investigations. Metal-doped CQDs were prepared using several metal precursors: iron (2+ and 3+), magnesium (2+), copper (2+), sodium (1+) and zinc (2+). In all cases, the contra ion was sulfate. Scheme 1

TiO2 NPs were synthesized using a MW-assisted methodology with an aqueous ammoniacal solution and TALH as the titanium precursor. That methodology is reported in detail in previous works [4,23,24]. In brief, 5 mL of TALH was mixed with 45 mL of 0.1 mol L−1 of NH3 and irradiated for 15 minutes at 160°C using a Microwave Digestion System MARS 6 (CEM Corporation) [24].

TiO2 NPs (AEROXIDE® TiO2 P25) and TiO2 NPs synthesized by MW irradiation were subsequently impregnated with CQDs to produce CQDs@TiO2 nanocomposites through a conventional wet impregnation method. Briefly, fixed amounts of TiO2 P25 (200 mg) and TiO2 via MW (250 mg) were mixed at different mass proportions of the CQDs as-synthesized solution ranging from 0 to 10. After that, the mixtures were suspended on 50 mL of deionized water, stirred overnight, and filtered. Finally, the solids were dried in an oven (50 °C) for 12 h and stored until utilization. The nanocomposites are named as Cu-CQDs@P25TiO2 or Cu-CQDs@MWTiO2 when the CQDs were impregnated on commercial P25 TiO2 NPs or MW prepared TiO2 NPs respectively.

The optical properties of the prepared TiO2 NPs were measured using a double-beam CARY 5000 spectrophotometer (Varian) in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm containing an integrated sphere. The bandgap of the samples were calculated using the Kubelka–Munk model assuming that the sample scattering coefficient was constant for the UV–Vis wavelength range.[31] The UV−vis absorption spectra of the CQDs solutions were acquired on a UV-Vis Varian Cary 50 spectrophotometer. Steady-state fluorescence spectra were obtained using a Varian Fluorescence Cary Eclipse instrument at the excitation wavelength of 390 nm. Quartz glass high performance 200−2500 nm cuvettes (10 mm optical pass) were used.

The nanostructures were also characterized by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and Energy Dispersive X-Ray Analysis (EDX) using the FEI TECNAI F20 (Netherlands) microscopes operating at 200 kV. The samples were drop-casted over 400 mesh carbon coated Ni grids, allowing EDX analysis of Cu on the samples. The mean size of the NPs was measured using the software ImageJ®.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to confirm the presence of CQDs on the pristine TiO2 NPs. XPS spectra measurements were carried out using Al Kα radiation as the excitation source and a conventional electron spectrometer (Omicron Gmbh, Germany). It was equipped with a high performance hemispherical energy analyzer and a seven-channeltron detector. The pass energies of 50 eV and 10 eV were used for recording the surveys spectra and the selected atomic signals respectively. In the spectra, the position of the C–C/C–H was specified, and other peaks of different carbon environments were fixed relative to this peak, set at 285.0 eV. The Casa XPS software package was used for the deconvolution of the selected atomic regions. The XPS signals were analyzed and peak-fitted after the subtraction of a Shirley background using Gaussian-Lorenzian peak shapes.

Hydrogen evolution experiments were performed using a Teflon reactor of (24.16±0.01) mL with a quartz window of 2.54 cm in diameter. Methanol was used as sacrificial agent with a methanol/water solution proportion of 2/15 v/v. Photogeneration experiments were carried out by the dispersion of the photocatalyst (1 mg/mL) in the methanol/water solution. Additionally, the water spitting reaction without methanol in only distilled water was also investigated. The reactor was deaerated with nitrogen for 30 minutes prior to irradiation. A high pressure Xe/Hg lamp (Sciencetech Inc. 140 W) was used for the photocatalysis experiments allowing the irradiation of the samples under a wide spectral range. The lamp power used was 100 W. The photocatalytic activity was evaluated by gas chromatography using a Shimatzsu GC-2010 equipment with a molecular sieve 5 Å packed column as previously reported [32,33].

For the photodegradation experiments aqueous solutions of 35 mL IC (30 ppm) in a quartz photochemical reactor were irradiated at several time intervals. A Long Wave Pass CUT-ON Filter, 25.4 mm, 400 ± 5 nm (446–1200 nm) from Newport Corporation was used when only visible light was allowed to enter into the reactor. The changes in concentrations of the dye were monitored regularly by measuring the absorbance at the correspondent maximum absorption wavelength of 610 nm.

Several cations were evaluated as reaction promoters for the synthesis of CQDs, i.e. H+, Na+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Cu2+, and Zn2+. The CQDs syntheses were carried out using the same anion (SO4 2-) and the same concentration of the cationic species. Fig. 1a shows that the fluorescence intensity was strongly dependent on the cation type. The absence of PL emission for the Fe2+ and Mg2+ ions suggests that CQDs were not formed under MW irradiation. Among the other tested ions, the PL emission was maxima when Cu2+ ions were used and minima for the Zn2+ ions. CQDs were not formed in the absence of the cation promoters. Fig. 1b shows the dependence of the photoluminescence of CQDs on the CuSO4 concentration. Optimization of experimental conditions is necessary to achieve CQDs with excellent fluorescence properties. The initial CuSO4 concentration was varied from 0.5 mmol/L to 10 mmol/L. As can be observed in Fig. 1b, the CQDs displayed the highest photoluminescence at 2.5 mmol/L of CuSO4. At lower or higher CuSO4 concentrations the PL decreased. The decrease in PL at lower CuSO4 concentrations of the promoter can be understood due to the lower efficiency of CQDs syntheses. The PL decrease at higher CuSO4 concentrations may due to quenching processes. The decrease in the PL observed at higher ratios was also explained as an occlusion effect of the surface defects by Cu excess in the ensemble [34]. Xu, Q. et al., have studied in some detail the copper doped carbon dots (Cu-CQDs) via a one-step hydrothermal approach as a function of temperature and reaction time. They found that the as-synthesized Cu-CQDs exhibited higher PL efficiencies at 200 °C and after 6 h of preparation conditions. The boiling point of Glycerol at 1 bar is 290 °C and in the present work the optimum MW irradiation time was 130 s.

Our results demonstrate the benefit of using the green MW methodology to prepare CQDs. In particular, the as-prepared Cu-CQDs exhibited stable PL up to 30 days, which was the maximum time measured (see Fig. S1). Additionally, the MW-prepared Cu-CQDs showed the highest PL emission compared to the other cation promoters at the same concentrations. For those reasons, Cu-CQDs were employed in the present work.

Photoluminescence of the Cu-CQDs was also studied, applying different light excitation wavelengths from 350 to 390 nm. Fig. 2 shows that the maximum emission signal of the Cu-CQDs shifted to longer wavelength from 455 to 481 nm with simultaneously slight reduction on the PL intensity with the increase in the excitation wavelength. These PL results were widely observed in carbon materials and explained as an excitation-dependent phenomenon type, called down-conversion behavior as a consequence of surface defect states near the CQDs Fermi level [35,36]. According to the results presented in Fig. 1 and Fig. 2, cupric ion was chosen as the reaction promoter for subsequent investigation.

The Cu-CQDs were characterized using HRTEM for the morphology and size distributions. Fig. 3a shows the typical TEM image of the as-prepared CQDs by MW irradiation, which exhibits a narrow size distribution with an average diameter of 3.7 nm (Fig. S2). Nearly 50 % of the CQDs have a diameter equal or smaller than 2.7 nm. Aiming to confirm the doping nature of the copper ions in the CQDs, EDX measurements were carried out (Fig. 3b and Table S1). In Fig. 3b two regions were selected for the EDX analysis (EDX1 and EDX2). In EDX1 less than about 50 % of the area contains CQDs; on the contrary in EDX2 almost all the area selected contains CQDs. It's possible to detect the presence of the elements C, O, Cu, and Ni in the EDX spectra. Ni originates from the grid, while O and Cu come from the sample itself. Carbon signal can originate from both the sample and the grid, as the grid is composed of Ni/C. It's important to note that this analysis is semi-quantitative, providing more reliable information, especially regarding Cu, than the other present elements. Region 1 has 1.63 ± 0.20 at% of Cu and region 2 presents 1.80 ± 0.23 at% of Cu (see Table S1). These results suggest that the Cu EDX signal comes mainly from the CQDs. It is possible to conclude that the present simple green methodology using MW can produce doped Cu-CQDs in a similar way that previous works have reported using traditional heating syntheses methods. From the HRTEM image (Fig. 3c-d), the lattice fringes of CQDs represented an interplanar distance of ∼0.30±0.1 nm, which agrees with the (002) spacing of graphitic carbon [37–39].

Once the Cu-CQDs were successfully obtained, TiO2 (P25) NPs were impregnated with the Cu-CQDs. To analyze this surface impregnation, XPS measurements were carried out, and the results are presented in Fig. 4. The survey XPS spectrum (Fig. 4a) shows the characteristic signals of the presence of Cu-CQDs@P25TiO2: C1s (33.2 %), O1s (46.1 %) and TiO2 2p (20.7 %). Although the presence of adventitious carbon cannot be excluded the amount of C 1 s is lower than usually observed as adventitious carbon. Additionally, the O/Ti ratio of 2.2 is very close to the theoretical ratio of TiO2 showing a low content of oxygen linked to the adventitious carbon. Fig. 4b shows typical peaks attributed to the C–C (probably graphite)/C-H, C–O and OC–O bonds, suggesting the presence of the CQDs in the Cu-CQDs@P25TiO2 [40–42]. The O 1 s signal deconvolution (Fig. 4c) is also quite informative. It shows the presence of O–Ti, O–C and O–H bonds adding evidence to the presence of Cu-CQDs@P25TiO2 [40,43]. The Ti 2p signals at 458.8 eV (2p3/2) and 464.5 eV (2p1/2) with a splitting of 5.7 eV were very close to the one expected for pure TiO2 (Fig. 4d) [44]. Copper signal was not detected by XPS analyses even after a soft sputtering with Ar+ ions (see Fig. S3).

Photoabsorption is one of the key factors that improve photocatalytic performance, particularly, the visible light absorption. Fig. 5 shows the corresponding spectra of crystallised pure TiO2 NPs and Cu-CQDs@MWTiO2. The TiO2 NPs were prepared by MW irradiation as previously reported using TALH as source of titanium [24]. From Fig. 5, an intense absorption band can be observed at 3.16 eV. That is indicative of crystalline semiconducting TiO2 with a typical bandgap of the anatase phase (3.2 eV) [45], while Cu-CQDs@MWTiO2 nanocomposites show an intense absorption band in the visible light region ranging from about 410–600 nm. The favorable visible-light absorption capacity and its bandgap energy was determined to be 1.96 eV from the intercept of the tangent in the plot of (ahν)1/2 versus photon energy (hν) according to Kubelka-Munk function [46]. The Cu-CQDs@P25TiO2 show a TiO2 bandgap of about 3.0 eV similar to the Degussa P25 of 3.1 eV [46] plus the visible light absorption of the composite. The impregnation of TiO2 P25 with the CQDs was also confirmed by UV–visible DRS spectra (see Fig. S4).

In a previous work, Wang et al. prepared TiO2 NPs-graphene quantum dots (GQDs) composites by exfoliating carbon black in mixed acids [47]. They have observed that the bandgap can be controlled to extend it into the visible light range, depending on the GQDs size. They showed evidence that the improvement in visible light absorption was enabled by the Ti–O–C bond formed between GQDs and TiO2, calling the mechanism “quantum-confined bandgap narrowing”. TiO2 particles with impregnated GQDs of 0.9 nm, 1.4 nm and 2 nm in diameters presented bandgaps of about 3.2 eV, 2.5 eV and 1.8 eV respectively. In that communication, the authors proposed a quantum confinement feature, where the larger the size of GQDs in the GQDs-TiO2 composites, the further the red-shift of the bandgaps, extending the absorption spectrum to the visible range. In the present work, the resulting CQDs exhibited a narrow size distribution, with an average diameter of 3.7 nm with nearly 50 % of them smaller than 2.7 nm. The bandgap obtained in the Cu-CQDs@MWTiO2 composites was 1.96 eV very close to the GQDs-TiO2 composites. Wang et al. also observed photocatalytic degradation of a dye with only visible light irradiation, as in the present work (see Section 3.3). Therefore, a similar quantum confined bandgap mechanism can be invoked in the present work, in which additional energy states are formed below the conduction bands and above the valence bands of TiO2 and CQDs respectively. The final result is the reduction of radiative electron–hole recombination.

The photocatalytic activity of the CQDs@TiO2 was first evaluated by the evolution of H2 from methanol aqueous solutions under UV-Vis light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 6a, under UV-Vis light irradiation, the Cu-CQDs@P25TiO2 (1:0.5) nanocomposite exhibits the highest rate of photocatalytic H2 evolution compared with all the other Cu-CQDs@P25TiO2 samples. The Cu-CQDs@P25TiO2 (1:0.5) photocatalyst has more than 6 times activity than Cu-CQDs@P25TiO2 (1:0.25). The low content of impregnated CQDs on the P25 photocatalyst may lead to lower activity. Further, the increase in the impregnation of TiO2 NPs (P25) with the CQDs resulted in a decrease of H2 evolution rate, probably due to CQDs light scattering effects and the reduction of the TiO2 NPs catalytic active sites [9].

Fig. 6b shows the results of H2 photogeneration when TiO2 NPs were prepared by MW irradiation and then impregnated with the Cu-doped CQDs. The H2 evolution for Cu-CQDs@MWTiO2 is about 1.5 times higher than pristine TiO2 MW NPs. However, comparing with the commercial impregnated TiO2 P25 NPs (Fig. 6a) the H2 evolution rate of the Cu-CQDs@MWTiO2 (1:2; 1:4) is about 50 % higher than using the Cu-CQDs@P25TiO2. The higher amount of H2 produced with TiO2 NPs prepared by the MW method can be the result of the smaller size of the TiO2 NPs (5±1 nm) [24] compared with the commercial TiO2P25 NPs.

The oxygen evolution reaction (OER) is one of the half parts of the hydrogen fuel cell that generates H2 and also in the WS reaction. The OER process is slow and inefficient because it needs a high overpotential to produce O2 from water, evolving from a four-electron-transfer process into an O−O covalent bond. However, Fig. 6c shows more than 12 folder increase in the O2 compared to H2 evolution reaction (HER) using the Cu-CQDs@P25TiO2 photocatalyst at the 1:0.5 ratio (TiO2P25:CQDs). Table 1 presents the experimental results of the H2 and O2 evolution rates for the Cu-CQDs@P25TiO2 samples irradiated with UV–visible light. OER reaction rate is higher than HER one, except for the impregnation ratio 1:10 that is 0.9. It is worth noting that the prepared photocatalysts did not show evidence of visible light activity when irradiated with λ ≥ 400 nm for H2 and O2 evolution. Yu, H. et al. have concluded that the HER reaction occurs in CQDs impregnated on P25 when the light irradiation was higher than 450 nm due to the photosensitization effect of the CQDs [40]. Fig. 2 shows that the PL spectra of the Cu-CQDs at varying excitation wavelengths have high UV absorption contributions. The high absorption in the UV range is a common up-convertion property of CQDs [48,49]. The excitation energy of 390 nm is close to the visible limit (400 nm) and represents less than 10 % contribution compared to the other excitation energies studied in this work (see Fig. S5). The high UV absorption of the CQDs evidently increased the light responsive activity in the UV region [49] compared to the visible region and it would need much longer irradiation times to detect visible photocatalytic activity at λ ≥ 400 nm for H2 and O2 photogeneration. On a hydroxylated Ti-oxide surface at least two possibilities may exist for the localization of the holes: they may be localized on the Ti site generating high-valent oxo species, M(IV) = O; or on the oxygen atom leading to oxyl species with some Ti reduction (Ti(III) - O•) [50]. The generation of oxygen atoms with electron density deficiency on the surface region plays an important role in the OER. It may lead to a nucleophilic reaction of water [51]. Finally, Fig. 3a-b shows the presence of copper in the CQDs. Previous works have reported the influence of surface copper species on the H2/O2 photogeneration [52–54] and CO2 photoreduction [55,56] efficiencies. It was suggested that the Cu NPs in several oxidation states serve as the active site for photocatalytic reduction/oxidation processes. In the present work, no evidence of the presence of surface copper was detected by XPS analyses even after soft sputtering (see Fig. S3). It may indicate that the high photocatalytic activity observed here in the composites is a result of the decrease in the rate of radiative electron-hole recombination (QCBN), increasing the synergy in the charge transfer between the CQDs and the TiO2 NPs.

In recent years, the CQDs nanocomposites have shown excellent catalytic activity toward oxygen evolution. That is because the CQDs can protect the metal oxide and oxidation during water splitting, keeping simultaneously a high electronic conductivity of the nanocomposites [57]. In spite of the large majority of works were carried out using electrochemical approaches, some studies have obtained interesting results at very low overpotentials, such as the report on Mn-based oxides/oxyhydroxides and N-CDs [58]. The authors obtained large-size MnOOH@CDs0.2 nanorod that showed excellent OER activity with an overpotential of only 302 mV, achieving a current density of 10 mA cm−2. Furthermore, CQDs-based nanocomposites doped with metals, such CQDs from FeTiO3 perovskite [42], Co phosphide/CQDs composite [59], Co and Fe co-doped CQDs [60] have presented evidence of improving the OER performance. Fig. 6c shows that the Cu-doped CQDs prepared in the present work can favor the OER. It was hypothesized that the high electronic conductivity of the small Cu-CQDs on the TiO2 NPs allows a more efficient electron transfer for the photogeneration of oxygen [58]. To test the OER activity of the prepared photocatalyst on the WS reaction and simultaneously its recycling properties, some runs were carried out with only deionized water. The results presented in Fig. 7 show important preference for the OER compared to HER. The rate of the OER is much higher than the rate of HER. Additionally, the Cu-CQDs@P25TiO2 showed excellent recycling properties that led to a constant generation of Oxygen in three consecutive photocatalytic cycles.

Trying to confirm photocatalytic visible light activity, the as prepared Cu-CQDs@P25TiO2 were evaluated through photodegradation of the IC dye under only visible light irradiation (λ ≥ 400 nm). After the suspension reached the adsorption-desorption equilibrium, it was irradiated to investigate the photocatalytic reaction. Previous reported results by our research group, on the photodegradation of IC aqueous solution under visible light (λ ≥ 400 nm) in the presence of pure TiO2 showed no change in the IC UV-Vis spectrum [61]. Those results demostrated that there was not any electron transfer process after photon excitation of the IC with visible light to the conduction band of the pristine semiconductor when the IC was adsorbed on the TiO2 photocatalyst. Fig. 8 shows that the IC did not degrade with visible light in the absence of the photocatalyst. On the contrary, the presence of the Cu-CQDs@P25TiO2 led to a total decoloration of the IC solution in less than 3 h of irradiation. The rate of the heterogeneous photocatalytic degradation of a dye can be described with the Langmuir-Hinshelwood (LH) mechanism [62]. The value of apparent pseudo-first-order reaction rate constant obtained from the slope of the linear curve shown in Fig. 8 was 6.87 ×10−3 min−1, following the LH mechanism for the photodegradation of the IC. These results confirmed that the discoloration of the IC was promoted by the electron transfer from the Cu-doped CQDs to the TiO2 conduction band under only visible light illumination. The π conjugated CQDs in the Cu-CQDs@P25TiO2 acts as photosensitizer like the organic dyes [63]. Similar results were recently reported using FeTiO3/CQDs hybrid catalysts fabricated via ultrasonication followed by impregnation of CQDs. It was demonstrated an efficient charge transfer from CQDs to FeTiO3, providing additional boost for photocatalytic degradation under visible light irradiation of ciprofloxacin antibiotic [42].

We have demonstrated a rapid, environmentally friendly method for producing CQDs using microwave irradiation with a standard commercial microwave oven. A high dielectric solvent glycerol, a by-product of the biodiesel, was used as an efficient medium for absorption of the MW radiation. By incorporating copper ions as promoters, Cu-CQDs were synthesized in just 130 seconds of MW irradiation using a commercial microwave oven. The resulting CQDs exhibited a narrow size distribution, with an average diameter of 3.7 nm with nearly half of them smaller than 2.7 nm. Cu-CQDs were loaded onto TiO2 NPs. It results in a Cu-CQDs@P25TiO2 or Cu-CQDs@MWTiO2 nanocomposites with photocatalytic properties for hydrogen-oxygen evolution and photodegradation of IC under UV–visible and only visible light irradiation, respectively. An efficient OER activity was measured under UV–visible irradiation of the Cu-CQDs@P25TiO2 nanocomposite. It is hypothesized that the high electronic conductivity of the small Cu-doped CQDs can facilitate more efficient electron transfer during oxygen photogeneration. This work is expected to provide new possibilities, cost-effective, and simple synthetic methods for the preparation of CQD-doped metal oxide composites. It may provide powerful pathways for green chemical applications, realizing the potential of carbon materials. Strategies to scale up MW syntheses have been recently developed based on parameters such as absorbed density power and energy. However, in industrial microwave applications, such as continuous-flow microwave synthesis, there are several challenges in reactor designs, optimization, and MW frequency to increase penetration. Many efforts are currently under way to overcome those challenges and gain a full understanding of the advantages and disadvantages of each setup.

Claudio W. Backes: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Elson Longo: Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition. Marcelo Assis: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Guilherme B. Strapasson: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Felipe B. Reis: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Daniel Eduardo Weibel: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Assis acknowledges the Ministerio de Ciencia e Innovación (Spain) for the Juan de la Cierva fellowship with code JDC2022-049934-I.
